summary: Through an analysis of Mary Wollstonecraft's personal letters, this article reveals her deep anxieties about debt, property, and dependence. It highlights her belief that true freedom and virtue for women require financial independence and the ability to earn their own subsistence, warning against the corrupting nature of financial obligation and patriarchal marriage.

principles.statement: True independence requires earning one's own subsistence.
principles.rationale: Depending on others for survival corrupts virtue, leads to manipulation, and destroys the delicate balance of equality required for true companionship.
principles.source_quote: I only desire to subsist, without being dependant on the caprice of an fellow creature

principles.statement: Financial obligation destroys personal freedom and dignity.
principles.rationale: Receiving obligations from others acts as a shackle that debases the mind and forces one into a subservient position.
principles.source_quote: Every obligation we receive from our fellow-creatures is a new shackle, takes us from our native freedom, and debases the mind, makes us mere earthworms – I am not fond of grovelling!

principles.statement: A quiet, composed life is preferable to the bustling, fashionable world.
principles.rationale: The fashionable world is full of dissipation and trivial concerns; true peace comes from a settled frame of mind and domestic pleasures.
principles.source_quote: I have no hope nor do I endeavour to attain any thing but composure of mind and that I expect to attain in some degree inspite of the storms and cross winds of life

mental_models.name: Equal Interdependence
mental_models.description: The idea that true companionship and equal citizenship can only exist when individuals are financially and morally independent of one another, rather than one being subservient to the other.
mental_models.example: Wollstonecraft striving to form an "all-female family" or household with her sisters and Fanny Blood, where they could earn a guinea a week and be independent together.

mental_models.name: The Web of Property
mental_models.description: Viewing property and inheritance laws not just as wealth, but as a system of power that traps women in dependence and vulnerability while protecting men's interests.
mental_models.example: Her sister Eliza being trapped in an abusive marriage because her husband retained all legal rights and property, forcing Mary to orchestrate a desperate escape that cost Eliza her child.

heuristics: Struggle with any obstacles rather than go into a state of dependance.
Live independent or not live at all.

signature_quotes.text: My debts too haunt me like furies
signature_quotes.context: Written in a letter to George Blood, describing how her financial obligations inhabited the same space as her grief.

signature_quotes.text: Every obligation we receive from our fellow-creatures is a new shackle, takes us from our native freedom, and debases the mind, makes us mere earthworms – I am not fond of grovelling!
signature_quotes.context: Expressing her disdain for debt and the loss of dignity it brings.

signature_quotes.text: It is a happy thing to be a mere blank, and to be able to pursue one's own whims, where they lead, without having a husband and half a hundred children at hand to teaze and controul a poor woman who wishes to be free
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the cost of women's duty and her aversion to matrimonial ties.

signature_quotes.text: The weakness that makes a woman depend on a man for subsistence produces a kind of cattish affection which leads a wife to purr about her husband as she would about any man who fed and caressed her
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing how financial dependence on men corrupts women's virtue and affection.

signature_quotes.text: I only desire to subsist, without being dependant on the caprice of an fellow creature
signature_quotes.context: Stating her ultimate goal of modest financial independence.

anti_patterns.description: Depending on a man for subsistence.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It produces a "cattish affection" where a woman merely purrs around the man who feeds her, destroying her virtue, equality, and native freedom.

anti_patterns.description: Following the fashionable crowd and focusing on "dissipation".
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Raising daughters to be accomplished and fashionable rather than "good and sensible" leaves them without true independence or moral substance.

anti_patterns.description: Founding happiness on money or social status.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It corrupts the heart and leads to broken promises, as seen when her lover Imlay abandoned her because a "certain situation in life" became more necessary to him than an uncorrupted heart.
